articles.title: A System in Motion: The Evolution of Orthopaedic Care in Romania.
articles.authors: Dragosloveanu S, Nedelea DG, Scheau C
articles.population: Romanian orthopaedic patients
articles.outcomes: Adoption of digital infrastructure and robotic-assisted surgery; hospital bed capacity; arthroplasty register modernisation
articles.take_home: Romania has increased its use of digital technology and robotic surgery in orthopaedic care, and has good hospital capacity for treating older patients and complex cases. The country has also updated its joint replacement register to match standards used elsewhere in Europe.

articles.title: Salvage total hip arthroplasty after internal fixation compared with acute total hip arthroplasty for fracture: a cohort study based on 32,960 cases from the Dutch Arthroplasty Register.
articles.authors: Van Marle L, Peters RM, Van Steenbergen LN, Jaarsma RL, Doornberg JN, Zijlstra WP
articles.study_type: Cohort study using national register data
articles.population: Patients treated with total hip replacement for hip fracture
articles.sample_size: 32,960 procedures (12,486 salvage replacements after failed internal fixation; 18,450 acute replacements)
articles.outcomes: Revision surgery rates at 1 and 5 years; reasons for revision including infection, loosening and dislocation
articles.take_home: Patients needing hip replacement after a failed internal fixation repair had similar revision rates to those receiving replacement as first-line treatment for hip fracture. Both groups had broadly similar outcomes, though patients requiring salvage replacement tended to be younger, heavier and in poorer health.

articles.title: Treatment and outcomes of fractures in children: a registry-based cohort study of 10,144 fractures.
articles.authors: Laaksonen T, Grahn P, Ahonen M, Ahola JA, Helenius I, Nietosvaara Y
articles.population: Children with fractures treated at a Level I trauma centre in Helsinki
articles.outcomes: Surgical timing, treatment methods, complication rates, unplanned reoperations, permanent nerve injuries, deep infections
articles.take_home: Children's fracture care at this hospital was safe and effective, with surgery performed promptly and permanent nerve injuries or infections being very rare. Most forearm fractures were successfully treated without general anaesthetic in the emergency department.

articles.title: Reliability and clinical utility of paediatric ankle fracture classifications : a systematic review.
articles.authors: Teo LS, Xiao L, Yeo BKK, Rayapati R, Lim AKS, Hoipo Hui J, et al.
articles.population: Paediatric ankle fractures
articles.outcomes: Interobserver and intraobserver reliability of classification systems, clinical utility for predicting premature physeal closure and angular deformity
articles.take_home: The Dias-Tachdjian classification system was most reliable for paediatric ankle fractures, whilst the Salter-Harris classification was most commonly used and best at predicting problems with growth. Using both systems together may help doctors plan treatment more accurately.

articles.title: Diagnostic value of serum troponin in stable patients at risk for blunt cardiac injury.
articles.authors: Cleary HL, Bernard MD, Davenport DL, Bernard AC
articles.population: Adult trauma patients with physician diagnosis of blunt cardiac injury or sternal fracture who were haemodynamically stable
articles.outcomes: Adverse cardiac events (new arrhythmia requiring treatment, cardiac surgery, or cardiac-related mortality)
articles.take_home: In stable patients admitted with possible blunt cardiac injury, serum troponin levels did not help predict who would have heart rhythm problems or other serious heart events. This suggests that stable patients may not need routine troponin testing, though abnormal heart tracings (ECG) were associated with increased risk.

articles.title: The fate of stage I Masquelet cement spacers in long bone reconstruction for acute fractures.
articles.authors: Dekle JK, Sisk CK, Litten RM, Alcaide DM, McIlwain RN, Wilson AL, et al.
articles.population: Adult patients with acute long bone fractures treated with Masquelet technique at a Level I trauma centre
articles.sample_size: 156 patients (48 Stage I only, 108 completed Stage II)
articles.outcomes: Stage II completion rates, fracture-related infection, hardware removal, wound dehiscence, amputation, mortality
articles.take_home: About one in three patients who had the first stage of Masquelet reconstruction did not go on to have the second stage, most commonly because of amputation. Patients who completed both stages and those who had only the first stage had similar complication rates and outcomes at follow-up.

articles.title: Efficacy and limitations of a web-based native adult knee septic arthritis calculator in urban trauma care.
articles.authors: Serbin RP, Burger J, Odum S, Chandler C, Welch S, Di Napoli N, et al.
articles.population: Adults with suspected knee septic arthritis undergoing arthrocentesis at a tertiary referral centre
articles.outcomes: Diagnostic accuracy of web-based calculator using Newman criteria as gold standard; optimal risk thresholds for surgical intervention and exclusion of septic arthritis
articles.take_home: A web-based calculator using six clinical and laboratory variables can accurately identify knee septic arthritis, with a calculated risk of 58% or higher suggesting surgery is needed and below 25% being reassuring that septic arthritis is unlikely. The tool performed very well in this hospital study, though further testing in other settings is still needed.

articles.title: External validation of machine learning models for estimation of mortality 1, 3, 6, and 12 months after hip fracture on 5,055 consecutive patients.
articles.authors: Mosfeldt M, Jorgensen HL, Lauritzen JB, Jansson KA
articles.study_type: External validation study of machine learning models
articles.population: Patients with hip fractures from 2 hospitals in Stockholm, Sweden
articles.outcomes: Mortality at 1, 3, 6, and 12 months after hip fracture, assessed using area under the receiver operating characteristic curve, calibration metrics, and decision curve analysis
articles.take_home: Three previously developed machine learning models for predicting death after hip fracture were tested in a new group of patients and performed well, with the XGB model being most accurate. The models were adjusted to account for differences in death rates between groups and are now available online to help doctors make personalised predictions for individual patients.

articles.title: CT-based micromotion analysis for tracking fracture displacement and fragment movement in trochanteric hip fractures: an agreement study.
articles.authors: Aberg H, Hanni M, Sandberg O, Jonsson KB
articles.study_type: Prospective cohort study with agreement analysis
articles.population: Patients with trochanteric hip fractures treated with cephalomedullary nail
articles.outcomes: CT-based micromotion analysis measurement agreement for fracture displacement, surgical reduction accuracy, and postoperative fragment movement; sub-millimetric repeatability for translation and less than 1 degree for rotation
articles.take_home: A new CT scanning method can precisely measure how hip fracture fragments move in three dimensions, with excellent repeatability. After surgery, fractures remained stable during healing with small predictable movements of bone fragments.

articles.title: Fear of reinjury is associated with failure to return to preinjury level of activity after operative repair of ankle fractures.
articles.authors: Hadley SM Jr, Peabody JJ, Durudogan EH, Westvold SJ, Bergman R, Chang S, et al.
articles.population: Patients who underwent operative repair of ankle fractures
articles.outcomes: Association between fear of reinjury and failure to return to preinjury activity level
articles.take_home: All patients who experienced fear of reinjury failed to return to their activity level before injury, compared with only 12% of patients without fear. Fear of reinjury appears to be a significant barrier to recovery after ankle fracture surgery, particularly for people who were more active before injury.

articles.title: What is the desired screw trajectory for fixation of noncontiguous posterior distal articular fractures associated with distal tibial shaft fractures?
articles.authors: Moon TJ, Blumenschein LA, Swetz AM, Nedder VJ, Ochenjele G, Wetzel RJ, et al.
articles.population: Patients with distal tibial shaft fractures and associated posterior distal articular fractures
articles.sample_size: 56 posterior distal articular fractures in 74 patients with distal tibial fractures
articles.outcomes: Optimal screw trajectory angles and starting points for percutaneous fixation of posterior distal articular fractures
articles.take_home: A standardised screw angle and starting point would only work correctly in about half of cases when viewed from one angle and three quarters when viewed from another, so surgeons should use CT scans to plan each patient's screw position individually rather than using one standard approach for all cases.

articles.title: CTA-detected incidence and descriptive fracture patterns of arterial injury in 539 surgically treated tibial plateau fractures.
articles.authors: Valderrama J, Narvaez F, Cancino J, Raffo JF, Bosselin R, Zamora F, et al.
articles.population: Patients with surgically treated tibial plateau fractures at a level I trauma centre
articles.outcomes: Incidence of arterial injury detected on CTA; fracture patterns associated with arterial injury
articles.take_home: Arterial injury was found in 1.11% of surgically treated tibial plateau fractures. Whilst certain fracture patterns appeared more common in cases with arterial injury, the small number of events means these findings should be treated as preliminary observations only.

articles.title: Risk Factors for Failure of Repair of Diaphyseal Tibial Nonunions: A Retrospective Cohort Study of 108 Patients.
articles.authors: Fan S, Wagner RK, Lehle CH, Musick AN, Gregg AT, Janssen SJ, et al.
articles.population: Adults aged 18 years or older with a broken tibia that failed to heal (diaphyseal tibial nonunion) treated with surgery
articles.outcomes: Need for additional surgery to achieve bone healing; radiographic evidence of bone healing (mRUST score of 11 or above)
articles.take_home: About 1 in 4 patients needed another operation to help their bone heal, though 9 in 10 eventually achieved healing on X-ray. Smoking, an initial open fracture, and bone defects increased the risk of needing more surgery, whilst a surgical technique called decortication reduced this risk.

articles.title: Rethinking the Lisfranc injury: Rationale for and development of a new classification system for Lisfranc injuries.
articles.authors: Cawley WCF, Hwang P, Mittal R, Zochowski Y, Darwish I, O'Keefe R, et al.
articles.study_type: Development and validation of a new classification system using retrospective case series data
articles.population: Patients with indirect Lisfranc injuries (midfoot injuries between the foot bones and bases of the toe bones)
articles.sample_size: 176 indirect Lisfranc injuries with CT scans available
articles.outcomes: Inter-rater reliability (agreement between different doctors classifying the same injury) and intra-rater reliability (consistency of one doctor's classifications)
articles.take_home: A new classification system called Suthersan showed much better agreement between doctors (0.95 versus 0.65) compared to the existing standard system, suggesting it may be more reliable for categorising these injuries and planning treatment.

articles.title: Injury and trauma care training in global health: A scoping review of costs and cost-effectiveness across resource settings.
articles.authors: Phan HT, Nguyen BT, Zhao T, Phung LT, Keakabetse TR, Dinh TTN, et al.
articles.study_type: Scoping review of costs and cost-effectiveness
articles.population: Injury and trauma care training programmes across low-, middle- and high-income countries
articles.outcomes: Implementation costs (ranging from USD 3.70 to USD 1433.50), cost-effectiveness of training programmes, cost drivers including foreign instructor involvement, and impact of training-of-trainers models on costs
articles.take_home: Most trauma care training programmes were found to be cost-effective, and using trainer-of-trainers and cascading models can reduce costs by up to 28% per participant without reducing training quality. However, the evidence on costs and cost-effectiveness remains inconsistent and needs more rigorous research, especially in regions with high injury burdens.

articles.title: Mechanism of injury not associated with acute compartment syndrome in Tibia fractures.
articles.authors: Egbe S, Marks D, Christiano AV, Erdman MK, Wolf JM, Strelzow J
articles.population: Skeletally mature patients with tibial fractures treated at a level 1 trauma centre
articles.outcomes: Incidence of acute compartment syndrome (ACS); associations between injury mechanism, fracture location, morphology, patient characteristics and ACS development
articles.take_home: Acute compartment syndrome occurred in only 2.1% of tibial fractures overall, and the type of injury (blunt versus gunshot) did not increase the risk. Patients with blood vessel injury were significantly more likely to develop compartment syndrome regardless of how the fracture occurred.

articles.title: Psychological outlook and recovery after orthopaedic trauma: Evidence and implications for Central India and other low-resource settings.
articles.authors: Salphale YS, Saodekar H, Kimmatkar N, Shinde G, Kothari P, Salphale AY
articles.population: Adults with orthopaedic trauma
articles.outcomes: Pain, disability, functional recovery, quality of life, psychological distress (depression, anxiety, post-traumatic stress)
articles.take_home: About one-quarter to one-half of orthopaedic trauma patients experience significant psychological distress, which worsens pain and disability regardless of injury severity. Adding simple psychological screening and culturally appropriate support to routine trauma care could improve recovery, especially in low-resource settings.

articles.title: Peterson vs. Salter Harris physeal fracture classification systems: How consistent are they?
articles.authors: Charles-Lozoya S, Cobos-Aguilar H, Valdez-Cordova C, de la Parra-Marquez ML, Manilla-Munoz E, Arriaga-Cazares HE, et al.
articles.population: Paediatric orthopaedic surgeons and orthopaedic residents
articles.sample_size: 100 radiographs classified by 10 observers (5 experts and 5 residents)
articles.outcomes: Intraobserver and interobserver agreement for Salter-Harris versus Peterson physeal fracture classification systems
articles.take_home: Both the Salter-Harris and Peterson classification systems showed similar and moderate-to-substantial reliability for classifying physeal fractures in children, with no significant difference between them. This suggests either system could be used clinically, though both require consistent training for best results.

articles.title: Do reduced screws have a significant impact on fixation in medial open wedge high tibial osteotomy? A comparative fatigue and stability analysis.
articles.authors: Ozkaya M
articles.population: High tibial osteotomy fixation models
articles.outcomes: Stiffness, deformation, tibial plateau displacement, stress levels, fatigue life under various loading conditions
articles.take_home: Using two screws instead of three in the upper part of the plate provides similar stability and deformation under normal loading, though it may have a shorter lifespan under backward-directed forces. Two screws could be a reasonable option to preserve more bone in younger patients, though the plate design may need further improvement.

articles.title: Moving beyond mortality measurements after road traffic injuries: Ten-year death risk after road traffic injuries.
articles.authors: Cherchiglia ML, Mougin V, Gray A, Wool EE, Malta DC, Melo APS, et al.
articles.study_type: Retrospective cohort study using record-linkage methodology
articles.population: Patients hospitalised for road traffic injuries in Brazil from 2000 to 2015, aged 15 and over, compared with those hospitalised for other reasons
articles.outcomes: All-cause and cause-specific mortality risk over 3 months to 10 years follow-up
articles.take_home: People hospitalised after road traffic injuries had more than three times higher risk of death over the following 10 years compared to people hospitalised for other reasons. Men aged 15-59 with motorcycle, vehicle or pedestrian injuries were at particularly high risk, and those with previous road traffic injuries also had increased risks of death from substance use, violence and suicide.

articles.title: Overutilization of helicopter emergency medical services compared to ground transport in moderate-severe penetrating abdominal trauma & associated outcomes.
articles.authors: Hus A, Brown A, Bundschu I, Prashar S, Kumar S, Indorewala Y, et al.
articles.population: Adult trauma patients (18+ years) in hemorrhagic shock with moderate-to-severe penetrating abdominal injuries requiring massive transfusion protocol
articles.outcomes: Primary: 24-hour and in-hospital mortality. Secondary: transfusion requirements, intervention rate, and complications
articles.take_home: Ground ambulance transport was associated with higher 24-hour mortality than helicopter transport, but when scene time was less than 10 minutes, outcomes were similar between the two transport methods. This suggests helicopter emergency services may be overused for some patients who could be safely transported by ground ambulance if transport time is short.

articles.title: Early postoperative anxiety is associated with patient-reported recovery after hip fracture: A PROMIS-based longitudinal cohort study.
articles.authors: Achebe C, Cureton R, Rothberg D, Higgins T, Marchand L, Haller J
articles.population: Adults aged over 55 years with hip fracture treated with surgery
articles.sample_size: 304 patients for physical function analysis, 285 for pain interference analysis
articles.outcomes: Achievement of clinically important improvement in physical function and pain interference within one year after surgery
articles.take_home: Patients with higher anxiety levels in the first month after hip fracture surgery were less likely to achieve meaningful improvements in physical function and pain relief during the first year. Early postoperative anxiety may be an important marker to identify patients at risk of poorer recovery.

articles.title: Association between neighborhood opportunity and incidence of pediatric trauma.
articles.authors: Monroy A, Tan JM, Arbogast H, Spurrier R, Nager A, Chaudhari PP
articles.population: Children under 18 years old with home zip codes in Los Angeles County
articles.outcomes: Trauma activations per 100,000 children; injury mechanisms, severity and disposition by neighbourhood opportunity level; mortality rate
articles.take_home: Children living in neighbourhoods with fewer opportunities had higher rates of traumatic injury, particularly from being hit as a pedestrian or cyclist, firearm injuries and assaults, whereas children in better-resourced neighbourhoods had more falls and sports injuries. This suggests injury prevention programmes should be tailored to the specific risks facing different neighbourhoods.

articles.title: Satisfaction with nursing care among Jordanian burn patients admitted to burn critical care units: A cross-sectional study.
articles.authors: Al-Ghabeesh S, Alnaeem M, Qaddoura N, Ali BA, Al-Masalha HA
articles.population: Adult burn patients admitted to a burn intensive care unit in Jordan for at least 48 hours
articles.outcomes: Patient satisfaction with nursing care measured using the Arabic version of the Patient Satisfaction with Nursing Care Quality Questionnaire
articles.take_home: Burn patients in intensive care reported moderate satisfaction with nursing care. Patients with higher income were more satisfied, whilst those with facial deformity were less satisfied, suggesting that tailored support addressing both practical and emotional needs could help improve patient experience.

articles.title: Flexible intramedullary nailing in the treatment of paediatric forearm fractures: 15-years' experience at a level one trauma centre, comparison of implant retention versus removal and cost-effectiveness analysis.
articles.authors: Ismayl G, Alabbas A, Howard A, Fishlock A, Sabouni MY
articles.population: Children with forearm fractures treated with flexible intramedullary nailing
articles.outcomes: Secondary surgery rates, indications for surgery, time to implant removal, patient and parent satisfaction, cost analysis
articles.take_home: Leaving the metal nails in place after a child's forearm fracture heals is safe and does not lead to more unplanned operations than removing them. Keeping the nails in also saves the NHS money.

articles.title: Association of Primary Trauma Care training with mortality in isolated traumatic brain injury: A sub-analysis of the PISTACHIO study in Vietnam.
articles.authors: Nguyen BT, Lam TT, Vu NHN, Pham TH, Do TD, Nguyen TTN, et al.
articles.population: Patients with isolated traumatic brain injury at two provincial hospitals in Vietnam
articles.outcomes: 24-hour and 30-day mortality rates
articles.take_home: After Primary Trauma Care training, mortality from isolated head injury fell from 6.7% to 1.8% at 24 hours and from 10.8% to 4.1% at 30 days, suggesting that early airway and breathing support may protect the brain in resource-limited settings. However, a high loss to follow-up rate (38.7%) means these results should be interpreted with caution.

articles.title: Cemented versus uncemented femoral stem fixation in hip arthroplasty for displaced femoral neck fractures in elderly patients: A systematic review and meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials.
articles.authors: Malki AT, Babtain HW, Alharbi DM, Shahhatalsayed AA, Alshaban RM, Almaghrabi YT, et al.
articles.study_type: Systematic review and meta-analysis of randomised controlled trials
articles.population: Elderly patients with displaced femoral neck fractures undergoing hip arthroplasty
articles.outcomes: Mortality, periprosthetic fracture, dislocation, and wound infection
articles.take_home: Cemented hip replacements were associated with lower death rates and substantially fewer broken bones around the implant compared with uncemented replacements, with no increase in hip dislocations or infections. Cemented fixation appears to be a safer and more reliable approach for elderly patients with femoral neck fractures.

articles.title: Impact of surgical timing on complications and mortality in periprosthetic hip fractures compared to native hip fractures.
articles.authors: Aerden LKP, Brouwers X, Sermon A, Ghijselings S, Vles G, Herteleer M, et al.
articles.population: Patients with periprosthetic hip fractures treated surgically at a university trauma centre, compared with a published native hip fracture cohort
articles.outcomes: Time to surgery, reasons for surgical delay, complication rates, length of stay, and mortality at 30, 60, 90 days and six months
articles.take_home: Periprosthetic hip fracture patients waited significantly longer for surgery than native hip fracture patients, mainly due to surgeon availability and logistical factors rather than patient-related issues. However, the surgical delay did not lead to more complications or worse overall survival, and periprosthetic hip fracture patients actually had better survival rates than native hip fracture patients.

articles.title: Superior survival, hemodynamics, and metabolic recovery using PEG-20k-based low volume resuscitation in a polytrauma hemorrhagic shock model in rats.
articles.authors: Khoraki J, Payne C, Lust D, Mangino MJ, Liebrecht LK
articles.study_type: Animal model study (rats with polytrauma and hemorrhagic shock)
articles.population: Adult male Sprague-Dawley rats weighing 250-400 grams
articles.outcomes: Survival time, blood pressure (MAP), blood lactate levels, and amount of bleeding
articles.take_home: A new type of fluid called PEG-20k helped rats survive much longer after severe trauma and blood loss compared to standard fluids, with better blood pressure and metabolic recovery. This shelf-stable fluid might be useful for treating trauma patients in remote areas or during long waits for hospitals where blood transfusions are not available.

articles.title: The forequarter lateral implosion injury: A diagnostic paradigm and Ch-Sh (chvarepsilon-sha) classification.
articles.authors: George A, Paull TZ, Winter JD, Levine J, Cole PA
articles.population: Patients with two or more rib fractures and ipsilateral shoulder girdle injury
articles.sample_size: 354 consecutive patients from a Level 1 trauma centre between 2018-2022
articles.outcomes: ICU admission, pneumothorax, pulmonary contusion, mechanical ventilation, spinal or other orthopaedic fractures, Injury Severity Score, 30-day mortality, and classification system reliability
articles.take_home: More severe chest wall injuries were linked to worse outcomes including higher ICU admission and death rates, but injuries affecting multiple shoulder structures together were associated with better outcomes than single scapula or upper arm fractures, possibly because the shoulder absorbs the impact force more effectively.

articles.title: More than meets the eye: the continuing importance of tertiary trauma survey even in moderate (ISS 9-15) trauma.
articles.authors: Knight C, Burke JR, Hind S, Smith SR
articles.population: Major trauma patients aged over 16 years admitted to a UK Major Trauma Centre
articles.outcomes: Incidence of unrecognised injuries identified at tertiary trauma survey, sensitivity and specificity of the survey, risk factors for unrecognised injuries
articles.take_home: Nearly one in five trauma patients had injuries that were not spotted in initial assessments but were found at a later detailed examination, particularly injuries to the face, head and eyes. This detailed examination was very good at finding these injuries and should be part of routine trauma care.

articles.title: Minimal Movement Restrictions Do Not Increase Hip Dislocations Following Total Hip Arthroplasty: A Before-and-After Study of 10,357 Patients.
articles.authors: Klaassen AD, Willigenburg NW, Musters JWQ, Jager J, Poolman RW
articles.population: Patients undergoing total hip arthroplasty between January 2015 and March 2020
articles.sample_size: 10,357 patients (7,666 with strict restrictions, 2,691 with minimal restrictions)
articles.outcomes: Incidence of hip dislocation within 90 days after surgery. Strict restrictions group: 1.46% (112 dislocations). Minimal restrictions group: 1.93% (52 dislocations). Difference was not statistically significant (p = 0.093).
articles.take_home: Patients who had minimal movement restrictions after hip replacement surgery did not have more hip dislocations than those with strict restrictions. This suggests that reducing movement restrictions after hip replacement may be safe and could help patients return to normal activities faster.
